skill.name: kling-3-0
skill.description: Use RunComfy CLI guidance to generate Kling 3.0 videos across Standard, Pro, and 4K tiers. The skill helps choose text-to-video or image-to-video endpoints, build valid inputs, tune prompts, and understand pricing, duration, audio, and output constraints.
skill.summary: Generate Kling 3.0 videos through RunComfy CLI endpoints.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives caused by markdown code spans, endpoint names, ellipsis in example URLs, and version text. The confirmed concerns are legitimate but elevated: the skill instructs users to run the RunComfy CLI, submit prompts and image URLs to RunComfy, store an API token locally, and download generated files. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration, hidden script, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: External CLI execution submits remote video jobs
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users or agents to run the RunComfy CLI with endpoint names and JSON inputs. This is expected behavior, but it executes a local command and sends generation requests through an external service, so users should review prompts, image URLs, cost, and output directories before running it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and workflow explicitly call runcomfy run and describe POST and polling behavior. The behavior is legitimate for the skill, but it is an elevated operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Credential and generated media handling require user trust
medium_findings.description: The skill documents token storage in a home configuration file and generated media downloads to a selected output directory. This is normal for the RunComfy CLI, but it means local credentials and potentially large generated files are involved.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly states where the token is stored and that generated outputs are downloaded. I found no evidence that the skill itself reads or exfiltrates the token.

low_findings.title: Markdown syntax caused static-analysis false positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged markdown backticks, model endpoint text, version numbers, and ellipsis examples as shell execution, weak cryptography, and path traversal. These are documentation examples and prose, not executable Ruby code or cryptographic routines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are markdown tables and example JSON fields. There is no Ruby code, no cryptographic implementation, and no filesystem traversal operation at these locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded documentation and service URLs are expected
low_findings.description: The skill contains RunComfy homepage, documentation, model, and endpoint references. These URLs are appropriate for a RunComfy integration and do not show suspicious data exfiltration by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are vendor documentation, model pages, and stated outbound service endpoints. Their presence matches the stated purpose of the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local command invocation pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses runcomfy run commands as the primary mechanism. Users should treat command execution as intentional and review arguments before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are shown directly in bash blocks. They are not hidden, but they are still external command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote API submission and media download pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill states that the CLI posts requests to RunComfy, polls for completion, and downloads generated media from RunComfy domains.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network workflow is described explicitly. I found no evidence of unauthorized endpoints beyond the documented RunComfy service domains.

content.value_statement: Video generation workflows need correct endpoint choice, input formatting, and cost awareness. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Kling 3.0 runs on RunComfy.
actual_capabilities: Selects among Standard, Pro, and 4K Kling 3.0 tiers.
Routes requests to text-to-video or image-to-video endpoints.
Shows required and optional input fields for all endpoint types.
Provides RunComfy CLI examples for each supported endpoint.
Explains duration, aspect ratio, audio, and pricing tradeoffs.
Documents token storage, network endpoints, and output downloads.
limitations: Requires an installed RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
Image-to-video requires publicly reachable HTTPS image URLs.
Each generation is limited to 15 seconds.
The skill provides guidance; the RunComfy service performs generation.

use_cases.title: Draft social video variants
use_cases.description: Use Standard text-to-video to test short vertical or square clips before spending on higher tiers.

use_cases.title: Animate product or character references
use_cases.description: Use image-to-video prompts to preserve a reference image while adding camera motion and atmosphere.

use_cases.title: Prepare finished cinematic clips
use_cases.description: Use Pro or 4K guidance for high-fidelity scenes with audio, controlled duration, and clear cost planning.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic draft clip
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a 5 second Kling 3.0 Standard text-to-video clip. Use a 16:9 aspect ratio and describe the RunComfy command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Animate a reference image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Kling 3.0 image-to-video with this HTTPS image URL. Preserve the subject identity, add gentle camera motion, and keep the duration under 8 seconds.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a multi-shot Pro scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Kling V3.0 Pro prompt with three numbered shots, synchronized ambience, and stable character identity. Explain cost and duration choices first.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a 4K production run
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Kling V3.0 4K generation plan for a finished brand clip. Include endpoint choice, JSON inputs, output directory, audio setting, and review risks.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a short vertical cafe video for social media.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Standard text-to-video, a 9:16 aspect ratio, a short duration, and a prompt focused on camera motion and ambience.

output_examples.input: A user provides a product image and asks for a premium animation.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends Pro image-to-video, restates identity anchors, checks that the image URL is public, and explains audio and cost choices.

output_examples.input: A user needs a native 4K brand film segment.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends the 4K endpoint, limits the scene plan to supported duration, and warns about larger downloads and higher cost.

best_practices: Start with Standard tier drafts before using Pro or 4K for final output.
Use clear camera, motion, subject, and audio directions in every prompt.
Review output directory, duration, audio, and cost before running the CLI.
anti_patterns: Do not request clips longer than 15 seconds in one generation.
Do not use private or inaccessible local image paths for image-to-video.
Do not combine too many conflicting styles, shots, or audio goals in one prompt.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps prepare and run Kling 3.0 video generation jobs through the RunComfy CLI.

faq.question: Does it support text-to-video and image-to-video?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers both modes across Standard, Pro, and 4K tiers.

faq.question: Do I need a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. You need the RunComfy CLI and either login credentials or a RUNCOMFY_TOKEN value.

faq.question: Can it use local image files?
faq.answer: No. Image-to-video requires a publicly fetchable HTTPS image URL.

faq.question: What is the main security consideration?
faq.answer: The skill runs a local CLI that sends prompts and image URLs to RunComfy and downloads generated media.

faq.question: Is this skill blocked from publication?
faq.answer: No. The audit found publishable medium risk with warnings, not confirmed malicious behavior.
